danger_signals.visual_form: On-screen hostile figures with weapons in first-person view
danger_signals.meaning: Active combat threat requiring player to shoot

danger_signals.visual_form: Visible bullets, grenades, mortar and bazooka rounds, missiles traveling toward player
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player health; can be shot down defensively

danger_signals.visual_form: Trucks, boats, helicopters, armored transports on-screen
danger_signals.meaning: Stage-specific threat requiring destruction to complete objectives

danger_signals.visual_form: On-screen damage bar filling due to enemy attacks or friendly fire
danger_signals.meaning: Player health degradation; stage failure or game over risk
Collateral hazard—destroys all targets on screen including friendlies

danger_signals.visual_form: Close-range hostile figures with melee weapons

safety_signals.meaning: Recovers player damage bar; indicates resource availability

safety_signals.visual_form: Shootable objects that replenish player resources
safety_signals.meaning: Sustains offensive capability; essential for stage progression
Unlimited ammunition and increased fire rate for 10 seconds; damage mitigation window
Hostages to protect and rescue; damaging them increases player damage bar

safety_signals.visual_form: Transition to interrogation sequence or next mission screen
safety_signals.meaning: Successful progression; health recovery and narrative advancement

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Roy Adams (player character perspective); military operative
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: First-person weapon view; targeting reticle; gunfire and grenade launcher mechanics

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Uniformed hostile figures; distinct from civilians by weapon presence and aggression
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Jungle, Prison Camp, Village, Airport, Concentration Camp environments; armed patrols; vehicle support

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Women, children, nurses, boys; regional variant: bikini babe (Japan only); visually distinct from soldiers
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Village and Prison Camp stages; require protection during rescue operations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Title screen with regional variations (Japan/US/Europe differ)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player selects mission order (Japanese version allows choice; US/EU linear)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Horizontal rail-scrolling through jungle terrain; enemy soldiers and vehicles appear
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player shoots required number of enemies to progress; horizontal scrolling movement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stage completion screen transitions to interrogation sequence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Roy Adams learns location of next objective (concentration camp, airport); narrative advancement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Civilian figures present on-screen alongside enemy forces
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must avoid shooting friendlies (increases damage bar); completion condition tracks rescued hostages

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Game over screen with contextual message (death vs. rescue failure)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Stage restart on continue; multiple ending variants depend on total hostages rescued

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: NES version displays multiple endings based on hostage count rescued
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Presidential satisfaction rating and bonus scaling by mission completion thoroughness

environmental_storytelling.technique: Stage-specific location design as narrative progression
environmental_storytelling.example: Jungle → Prison Camp → Village → Airport → Concentration Camp; each location represents escalating rescue operation complexity and reveals enemy infrastructure
Defeated soldier reveals next objective location; connects stages narratively without cut-scenes
Women, children, nurses present in all versions; bikini babe civilian variant (Japan only) reflects regional content preferences; indicates location is civilian-inhabited settlement

environmental_storytelling.technique: Mission order flexibility as strategic narrative
environmental_storytelling.example: Japanese version allows mission selection; player can complete fewer missions for lower ending satisfaction, reflecting operational choice consequences

environmental_storytelling.technique: Vehicle types as faction military capability
environmental_storytelling.example: Trucks, boats, helicopters, armored transports indicate different terrain and operational domains; suggests enemy has logistics and air support

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Patrol environments; attack with gunfire, grenades, mortars, bazookas, missiles; use knives at close range
Return gunfire; launch visible projectiles that can be shot down by player
Remain stationary or move within stage; cause player damage increase if shot; must be protected to complete stage
First-person perspective; fully automatic fire capability; grenade launcher function; health bar depletes on damage or friendly fire
Appear on-screen as required destruction targets; distinct from infantry threats; indicate enemy vehicle strength
Destructible; indiscriminate explosion damages all entities including friendlies; player must strategically use or avoid

notes: Operation Wolf is a rail-shooter that communicates world-state primarily through horizontal scrolling progression and on-screen NPC presence. Danger is signaled by enemy soldier and vehicle visibility, projectile trajectories, and damage bar feedback. Safety is indicated through collectible power-ups and civilian protection mechanics. Faction identity is minimal (no faction colors/symbols documented) but is distinguished through soldier vs. civilian visual contrast and hostage rescue narrative. Regional differences (Japan allows mission selection, includes bikini babe civilian) suggest localized content adaptation but do not alter core manifestation systems. Multiple endings based on hostage count create variable world-state outcomes reflecting player operational success.
